metadata_information.title: The Role of Skills and Gender Norms in Sector Switches : Experimental Evidence from a Job Training Program in Nigeria
metadata_information.version: Python script, from D&R API, generated on 2026-03-19

title_statement.title: The Role of Skills and Gender Norms in Sector Switches : Experimental Evidence from a Job Training Program in Nigeria

document_description.abstract: Industrialization and structural change entails shifting workers from low skill to high skill occupations, and in emerging economies a number of constraints may impede sectoral switches among workers, including skill and spatial mismatches, and social norms related to gender in the workplace. This study uses a job training experiment across five cities in Nigeria to estimate the overall effect of training on sectoral switches into the information and communications technology and business process outsourcing sector, and to examine the role of various factors that might constrain switching. After two years the treatment group was 26 percent more likely to work in the ICT-enabled service sector, although they were no more likely to be employed than the control group. Sector switches were higher among those with sector-relevant skills, and training magnified the skills premium in switching. Switches were also higher in some cities, despite large improvements in skills in all cities. Women who were implicitly biased against associating women with professional attributes were two times more likely to switch into ICT than unbiased women, suggesting that training helped overcome self-defeating social norms among female applicants of the program. These results suggest that training can be an effective strategy for inducing sector switches and overcoming social norms that hamper female mobility in the labor market, but that addressing spatial mismatches in labor demand and labor supply may require additional interventions.

document_description.disclaimer: The Policy Research Working Paper Series disseminates the findings of work in progress to encourage the exchange of ideas about development issues. An objective of the series is to get the findings out quickly, even if the presentations are less than fully polished. The papers carry the names of the authors and should be cited accordingly.
